templates.name: Brazil Bolsa Família Conditional Cash Transfers
templates.description: Scale targeted conditional cash transfers tied to school attendance and preventive care, with focus on the poorest households.
templates.target_audience: Social protection ministries and fiscal policy teams

interventions.indicator_name: Average Income After Taxes and Benefits
interventions.justification: Higher disposable income through transfers improves consumption smoothing and service uptake.
Expanded social transfer channels mirror CCT program scale-up.
Conditionality tied to attendance is designed to reduce out-of-school children.

expected_outcomes.primary: Poverty and inequality decline while household resilience improves.
secondary: Higher school continuation rates for adolescents
Stronger local demand multipliers in poor municipalities

evidence.source: The Lancet (2013); World Bank (2013); IMF WP/17/225
evidence.case_study: Brazil expanded Bolsa Família to ~14 million households and observed large reductions in extreme poverty and measurable mortality improvements in high-coverage areas.
citations: World Bank (2013). Bolsa Família: Brazil’s Quiet Revolution.
IMF Working Paper 17/225 (2017). Inequality in Brazil.

templates.name: Nordic Comprehensive Social Welfare Model
templates.description: Expand universal social spending, redistribution, and labor-market institutions to reduce disposable-income inequality while maintaining productivity.
templates.target_audience: Advanced-economy finance ministries and social policy agencies

interventions.justification: Higher social expenditure is a core mechanism in Nordic redistribution systems.

interventions.indicator_name: Average Income After Taxes and Benefits
interventions.justification: Tax-transfer design lifts disposable income for lower and middle deciles.
Institutional quality support improves policy implementation credibility.

expected_outcomes.primary: Lower disposable-income inequality with stronger social mobility outcomes.
secondary: Higher labor force attachment including women

evidence.source: NBER Working Paper 33444; OECD Society at a Glance 2024
evidence.case_study: Nordic economies sustain low Gini levels and high mobility through large social spending, universal services, and broad redistribution.
citations: Mogstad, Salvanes, Torsvik (2025). NBER WP 33444.
OECD (2024). Society at a Glance: Social Spending.
Corak (2013). Journal of Economic Perspectives.

templates.name: Bangladesh Pluralistic Health System Transformation
templates.description: Scale community-based outreach, targeted subsidies, and primary-level delivery capacity to improve maternal and child health outcomes.
templates.target_audience: Health ministries and NGO delivery networks

interventions.justification: Social support improves care-seeking and continuity for vulnerable households.

interventions.indicator_name: Average Social Insurance and Transfer Income
interventions.justification: Transfer and insurance income reduces financial barriers to utilization.

interventions.indicator_name: Average Income After Taxes and Benefits
interventions.justification: Improved disposable income supports nutrition and preventive care uptake.

expected_outcomes.primary: Maternal and under-5 mortality trends improve through community-based delivery and financial access.
secondary: Faster fertility transition via women-focused outreach

evidence.case_study: Bangladesh reduced maternal and child mortality substantially through pluralistic delivery (government + NGOs + CHWs) despite low per-capita spending.
citations: Chowdhury et al. (2013). The Lancet. DOI: 10.1016/S0140-6736(13)62148-0
El Arifeen et al. (2013). The Lancet. DOI: 10.1016/S0140-6736(13)62149-2
NIPORT et al. (2012). Bangladesh Maternal Mortality Survey 2010.

templates.name: Thailand Universal Coverage Scheme Expansion
templates.description: Tax-financed universal coverage with low point-of-service charges and strategic purchasing to reduce catastrophic expenditure.
templates.target_audience: Health financing agencies and ministries of finance

interventions.justification: General-revenue financing is central to UCS sustainability.

interventions.indicator_name: Average Social Insurance and Transfer Income
interventions.justification: Insurance and transfer channels reduce out-of-pocket exposure.

interventions.indicator_name: Average Income After Taxes and Benefits
interventions.justification: Disposable-income support improves treatment adherence and access.

expected_outcomes.primary: Catastrophic health expenditure and medical impoverishment decline materially.
secondary: Improved continuity of care for informal workers

evidence.source: Thailand UHC evaluations and synthetic control studies
evidence.case_study: Thailand reached full population coverage quickly and sharply reduced catastrophic spending and medical impoverishment under UCS.
citations: Tangcharoensathien et al. (2020). International Journal for Equity in Health.
Tangcharoensathien et al. (2018). The Lancet.
World Bank UNICO Series No. 20 (2012). Thailand UCS.

templates.name: Rwanda National Digital Transformation
templates.description: Expand broadband backbone, digital public services, and digital skills to increase service delivery and export-oriented ICT activity.
templates.target_audience: ICT ministries and digital transformation units

interventions.justification: Digital service adoption increases value added in services.
Digital literacy and training raise human-capital stock.

interventions.indicator_name: Government Services Cost Component (US Constant Prices)
interventions.justification: Digitized government workflows improve service throughput and cost efficiency.

expected_outcomes.primary: Public service delivery digitizes while ICT-enabled sectors expand.
secondary: Lower transaction costs for citizens and firms
Improved export competitiveness in ICT-enabled services

evidence.source: World Bank Rwanda Digital Acceleration documentation
evidence.case_study: Rwanda combined backbone rollout with e-government services and digital-skills investments to accelerate inclusion and service access.
citations: World Bank (2020). Rwanda Economic Update: Digital Transformation.
World Bank (2021). Rwanda Digital Acceleration Project PAD.
WTO/World Bank (2024). Digital Trade for Africa: Rwanda.

templates.name: Estonia End-to-End E-Governance Ecosystem
templates.description: Implement interoperable digital identity, once-only data rules, and secure government data exchange for full-service digitization.
templates.target_audience: Public administration and digital identity authorities

interventions.justification: Information ecosystem quality supports digital trust and adoption.
Judicial/institutional capacity supports rule enforcement in digital state operations.
Lower embezzlement risk reflects stronger digital controls and traceability.

interventions.indicator_name: Government Services Cost Component (US Constant Prices)
interventions.justification: Digitized workflows reduce admin frictions in service delivery.

expected_outcomes.primary: Higher state capability with faster, lower-friction public services.

evidence.case_study: Estonia scaled national e-ID and interoperable data exchange to make nearly all public services digitally accessible.
citations: Vassil (2016). Estonian e-Government Ecosystem. World Bank background paper.
OECD (2015). Estonia and Finland digital services review.
Kattel & Mergel (2019). Estonia’s Digital Transformation.

templates.name: Kenya Mobile Money Financial Inclusion
templates.description: Scale mobile-money infrastructure and agent ecosystems to improve household resilience, inclusion, and small-enterprise activity.
templates.target_audience: Central banks, telecom regulators, and financial inclusion units

interventions.justification: Mobile financial services increase service-sector activity and formalization.
Improved liquidity and payments channels support labor income stabilization.

interventions.indicator_name: Economic value of women's skills and education (current US$)
interventions.justification: Women’s financial inclusion raises productive human-capital returns.

expected_outcomes.primary: Financial inclusion expands while household shock resilience improves.
secondary: Lower payment frictions in domestic transfers

evidence.case_study: Kenya’s mobile-money expansion lifted large numbers of households from extreme poverty and supported women’s transition into business activity.
citations: Suri & Jack (2016). Science. DOI: 10.1126/science.aah5309
FinAccess Household Survey (2019). CBK/KNBS/FSD Kenya.
Jack & Suri (2014). American Economic Review.

templates.name: Vietnam Export-Led Industrialization Bundle
templates.description: Combine trade liberalization, FDI-enabled manufacturing expansion, and industrial upgrading to accelerate structural transformation.
templates.target_audience: Trade ministries, investment agencies, and industrial policy teams

interventions.indicator_name: Total imports of goods and services
interventions.justification: Trade integration increases goods/services flows and global value-chain participation.
Manufacturing scale-up is central to export-led growth.
Produced and financial asset accumulation rises with sustained export growth.

expected_outcomes.primary: Faster GDP growth through manufacturing and export expansion.

evidence.source: IMF and World Bank evidence on Viet Nam reform era
evidence.case_study: Viet Nam’s post-Đổi Mới reforms sharply increased export intensity and manufacturing share while reducing poverty.
citations: Nguyen, Luu, Trinh. Brookings L2C WP No. 19.
World Bank WDI: Viet Nam exports (% GDP).

templates.cost_estimate_usd: multi-year trade and industrial policy package

templates.name: China-Style Mega Infrastructure Expansion
templates.description: Large-scale transport and capital infrastructure push to accelerate connectivity and output growth, with monitoring for diminishing returns.
templates.target_audience: Infrastructure ministries and national planning commissions

interventions.justification: Produced-capital accumulation is the direct infrastructure stock channel.
National wealth index rises with transport and logistics asset buildout.
Connectivity supports industrial output and agglomeration gains.

expected_outcomes.primary: Short-to-medium term growth acceleration from infrastructure capital deepening.

evidence.source: China HSR and infrastructure return studies
evidence.case_study: China’s sustained infrastructure wave coincided with large growth contributions and sharp transport accessibility gains, with later evidence of diminishing marginal returns.
citations: Lawrence, Bullock, Liu (2019). China’s High-Speed Rail Development. World Bank.
Deng & Shao (2022). Journal of Development Economics.
Hou, Li, Zhang (2020). Sustainability.

templates.cost_estimate_usd: 10-20% of GDP gross infrastructure investment window

templates.name: Ethiopia Road Sector Connectivity Program
templates.description: Expand and upgrade road networks to reduce transport frictions, improve market access, and lift consumption in connected regions.
templates.target_audience: Transport ministries and rural development agencies

interventions.justification: Road expansion directly raises produced-capital stock.
Network upgrades increase wealth through higher productive asset value.
Sustained public works spending is required for network expansion and maintenance.

expected_outcomes.primary: Rural access improves and poverty declines in newly connected areas.
secondary: Higher household consumption growth in treated zones

evidence.source: World Bank and IGC analyses of Ethiopia road program
evidence.case_study: Ethiopia’s road expansion period is associated with measurable consumption and poverty improvements in connected localities.
citations: World Bank (2015). Ethiopia transport systems support.
Shiferaw et al. (2012). IGC Working Paper.
World Bank (2020). Rural Roads, Poverty, and Resilience: Ethiopia.

templates.name: India Green Revolution Modernization
templates.description: Increase agricultural productivity via technology adoption, irrigation support, and income stabilization for farm households.
templates.target_audience: Agriculture ministries and food security councils

interventions.justification: Agricultural wealth-finance improvements support input and technology adoption.
Middle-income farm households scale output and resilience with better financing.

interventions.indicator_name: Methane emissions from oil and gas leaks
interventions.justification: Modernized practices are paired with methane-intensity reduction targets.

expected_outcomes.primary: Food production and farm incomes rise with lower vulnerability to food shocks.

evidence.case_study: India’s cereal yield and output expansion under Green Revolution packages supported food self-sufficiency and broad poverty reduction.
citations: Hazell (2009). IFPRI Discussion Paper 00911.
Bakalis et al. (2021). Frontiers in Sustainable Food Systems.

templates.name: Germany Energiewende Energy Transition
templates.description: Accelerate renewable deployment and energy-system decarbonization while managing affordability and reliability trade-offs.
templates.target_audience: Energy ministries, regulators, and climate agencies

interventions.indicator_name: CO2 Emissions from Power Plants (million tons)
interventions.justification: Power-sector decarbonization is a core transition target.
Building-efficiency and electrification reduce stationary emissions.

interventions.indicator_name: Methane emissions from oil and gas leaks
interventions.justification: Methane leakage reduction complements clean-energy pathways.
Grid and renewable infrastructure adds long-lived produced capital.

expected_outcomes.primary: Electricity mix decarbonizes while economy-wide emissions trend downward.
secondary: Lower carbon intensity of power supply

evidence.source: German federal emissions and energy reviews
evidence.case_study: Germany materially increased renewable electricity share over the last decade while reducing total emissions relative to 1990 baselines.
citations: IEA (2020). Germany Energy Policy Review.
Umweltbundesamt. Renewable Energies in Figures.
Fell (2021). Renewable Energy and Environmental Sustainability.

templates.cost_estimate_usd: multi-decade program with high capital and transition costs

templates.name: South Korea Tertiary Education Expansion
templates.description: Scale tertiary enrollment and teacher quality with alignment to industrial upgrading and labor-market demand.
templates.target_audience: Education ministries and human capital strategy units

interventions.justification: Higher tertiary attainment supports advanced-industry skill demand.

interventions.indicator_name: Primary School Teacher Training and Support
interventions.justification: Teacher quality and support improve learning pipeline quality.
Reduced secondary-age exclusion expands progression to tertiary levels.
Human-capital stock growth reflects education expansion impacts.

expected_outcomes.primary: Human-capital deepening supports productivity and income growth.
secondary: Improved social mobility via education access

evidence.source: OECD and WDI records on Korean education transition
evidence.case_study: Korea’s tertiary expansion coincided with rapid industrial upgrading and sustained growth in high-skill sectors.
citations: OECD (2013). Education Reform in Korea.
OECD (2025). Education at a Glance: Korea.
World Bank WDI. Tertiary enrollment, Korea, Rep.

templates.cost_estimate_usd: 4-7% of GDP total education spending envelope

templates.name: India Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan Elementary Education
templates.description: Drive near-universal elementary enrollment with school expansion, teacher support, and dropout reduction interventions.
templates.source: UNESCO / Government of India / World Bank
templates.target_audience: Education ministries and state school systems

interventions.indicator_name: Boys Not in Elementary School (total number)
interventions.justification: Program target is direct reduction in out-of-school primary-age children.
Retention at lower-secondary transition is required for universal completion.

interventions.indicator_name: Primary School Teacher Training and Support
interventions.justification: Teacher training/support is essential for quality and attendance gains.
More transparent teacher hiring supports staffing expansion.

expected_outcomes.primary: Primary enrollment approaches universality with lower exclusion.

evidence.source: SSA implementation assessments and UNESCO reviews
evidence.case_study: SSA scaled school infrastructure and enrollment significantly across India, while highlighting a continuing quality challenge.
citations: UNESCO (2014). Sarva Shiksha Abhiyan: Promising EFA Practices.
Nandi et al. (2023). International Journal of Educational Development.
UNESCO (2014). Lessons from implementation of SSA.

templates.cost_estimate_usd: $7-10 billion national program over implementation cycle

templates.name: Georgia Post-Rose Revolution Anti-Corruption Reforms
templates.description: Implement high-velocity anti-corruption reforms across police, licensing, and public service delivery institutions.
templates.target_audience: Anti-corruption agencies and public administration reform teams

interventions.justification: Directly target executive embezzlement and theft through enforcement and institutional redesign.
Judicial compensation/integrity improvements support credible enforcement.
Stronger media professionalism supports accountability and public scrutiny.

expected_outcomes.primary: Administrative corruption falls and state effectiveness rises.
secondary: Improved trust in core public institutions

evidence.source: OECD anti-corruption review and TI trend evidence
evidence.case_study: Georgia’s post-2003 reforms produced a rapid visible decline in petty corruption and major gains in business-environment indicators.
citations: OECD (2022). Anti-Corruption Reforms in Georgia.
World Bank Doing Business updates (2019).
Transparency International (2019). Georgia reform trajectory.

templates.cost_estimate_usd: institutional reform package with moderate fiscal cost

templates.name: Singapore Zero-Tolerance Anti-Corruption Framework
templates.description: Combine strict anti-corruption enforcement, meritocratic civil service incentives, and institutional accountability.
templates.target_audience: Executive offices, anti-corruption bureaus, and civil service commissions

interventions.justification: Aggressive enforcement and legal deterrence target executive theft and rent extraction.
Competitive and credible judicial/institutional compensation reduces corruption incentives.
Professional information ecosystems reinforce accountability norms.

expected_outcomes.primary: Sustained low corruption with high administrative credibility.

evidence.case_study: Singapore’s CPIB-centered system, coupled with civil-service incentives and enforcement credibility, maintained persistently low corruption over decades.
citations: Quah (2022). Public Administration and Policy.
De Graaf & Macaulay (2020). Anti-Corruption Agencies (Springer).
CPIB Singapore. Corruption Control Framework.

templates.cost_estimate_usd: low-to-moderate fiscal cost, high institutional commitment

templates.name: China WTO Accession and Trade Liberalization Package
templates.description: Use tariff and market-access reforms to expand trade flows, accelerate industrial output, and deepen global value-chain integration.
templates.target_audience: Trade ministries and macroeconomic reform units

interventions.indicator_name: Total imports of goods and services
interventions.justification: Lower trade barriers increase total cross-border goods and service flows.
Manufacturing scales with export-market access and FDI participation.
Wealth accumulation rises with prolonged export-led expansion.
External-liability pressure can fall with stronger export earnings.

expected_outcomes.primary: Trade integration drives higher output and productivity growth.
secondary: Stronger integration into global supply chains

evidence.case_study: Post-accession China experienced very large increases in trade volume and industrial output, with broad institutional adaptation to multilateral trade rules.
citations: WTO (2021). 20 years of China’s WTO membership.
Rumbaugh & Blancher (2004). IMF WP/04/36.
China and the WTO (2021). Cambridge University Press.
